record.title: Mahlon Follett, 1976-05-03, Interview - MP3 Recording
record.subject: Latah County -- Idaho
record.origin: Parents came from Minnesota in the 1880's
record.keywords: families presidents assassinations stores colleges and universities armed forces world wars roads automobiles women Native Americans dances rural communities sports clothing churches moonshine Prohibition Great Depression banks businesses drinking farming politics

point.title: He ran away to neighbors as a child; uncle went into dentistry to escape farming; father came home with news about McKinley's shooting
point.synopsis: Running away to the neighbor's as a child. His uncle went into dentistry to get out of farming; he found it to be isolated. Father hiked in from Lewiston; there was no fence on his route. Father came home with news of McKinley's shooting.

point.title: Early activity in Genesee; four general stores; barber shops
point.synopsis: Early activity in Genesee. Many more people lived in the surrounding area. Beginning of Follett store in 1896. A historical shoe invoice. Four general stores in Genesee. Six or seven worked in Follett's store. Saturday night business. Barber shop baths for hired men.

point.title: Attended Northwestern Business College in Spokane and worked at banks in Spokane and Genesee; enlisted during WWI
point.synopsis: He attended Northwestern Business College in Spokane and worked in banks in Spokane and Genesee. Serving in the army in New York City. They were treated very well; he enlisted with friends from Spokane. Solid support for First World War.

point.title: Started working at uncle's store instead of bank
point.synopsis: He decided that working for his uncle's store would get him ahead more quickly than the bank. Good roads and automobiles hurt the small town.

point.title: Decline of store in the twenties, intensified during the Deppression; description of what people bought
Credit and money in small town during the Depression; people paid more often but bought less
point.synopsis: Carrying farmers from year to year. Credit was the small town merchant's biggest headache. Money on books from the depression. Discounts from quick payment to merchandisers off set bank borrowing. Most people were trustworthy; it was hard to collect if they couldn't pay. Changing population in town. Some bought with the intention of never paying. In the depression people paid up better than they had before, while they bought less.

point.title: Failure of Genesee bank in the depression; Troy bank in Genesee was popular
point.synopsis: Failure of Genesee bank in the depression. Popularity of Troy bank in Genesee. Store's problem with paying creditors after bank closed.

point.title: Competition among grocers; more rivalry now
point.synopsis: The older Rosensteins didn't mingle with other towns people very much. Cooperation with other grocers; there was more rivalry in the earlier days. Some customers cited lower prices elsewhere.

point.title: Thrashing outfits coming in from Troy; women stayed in store; Indian trading
point.synopsis: Putting up orders. Threshing outfits came from Troy. High school kids "hung out" at Smolts. In the early days the women would stay around the store. Indian trade: they bought one item at a time.

point.title: Social life revived with dances; different church groups didn't associate until recently
point.synopsis: Social life - revival meetings were rather radical; dances in town and country. Visiting was similar in town and country. Different groups and churches didn't associate until recently. Community activities - horse show, ball games. Few farming families appeared to be "country people."

point.title: Genesee decline as business town due to location between Lewison and Moscow
point.synopsis: Genesee's decline as business town may come from its position between Lewiston and Moscow. Follett's tried to carry ready-to-wear clothes.

point.title: Revivals had little effect on most people's habits; more drinking during prohibition, mostly moonshine was bought
point.synopsis: Revivals had little effect on most people's habits. A baseball playing minister got people to be broader minded. Most moonshine was brought in; there was more drinking during prohibition because it was illegal. Political speakers drew crowds because there wasn't much else to do.

point.title: Vollmer's bank in Genesee didn't do much business because people didn't like Vollmer
point.synopsis: Vollmer's bank in Genesee didn't do much business because people didn't like Vollmer.

point.title: Moving Genesee from "old town" to new
point.synopsis: Moving Genesee from "old town" to new. Rosenstein would not have held the railroad up, as is often claimed, because he had to move his store.

point.title: Genesee as a thriving young town.
point.synopsis: Genesee as a thriving young town.
Hobo jungle at Genesee - dependability of men varied. Gradual decline of Genesee. He sold out business for a year, then bought it back.

record.description: Follett's store. Flourishing and decline of Genesee. Socializing in town. Problems with credit. Genesee banks. Moving of townsite. 5-3-76 2 hr
record.transcript: Download the PDF Index No transcript available.
record.rights: Digital format copyright: University of Idaho Library. Original Copyright: Latah County Historical Society
